evidence: Dependency direction is consistently top-down: utils ← config/query ← exploration ← api ← Main; no reverse imports found.
QueryExecutor is a proper interface in the query package; BlackLabQueryExecutor implements it and api/exploration only see the interface.
model and viz packages are pure leaf nodes with zero internal imports, functioning correctly as shared DTOs.
indexer.blacklab is fully isolated — no imports from query, api, config, or exploration; communicates only via filesystem artifacts.

RelationConfig.mergeLemmaConstraint + extractConstraintAttribute (config package) duplicates the pattern of PatternSubstitution.substituteCollocate + extractXposFromConstraint (api package): both parse a CQL bracket constraint, extract xpos/tag, and reconstruct a lemma-merged constraint. The logic cannot be unified without a boundary violation (config importing api), revealing a missing shared utility in utils/.

WordSketchApiServer instantiates SemanticFieldExplorer directly in its constructor (new SemanticFieldExplorer(executor)) rather than accepting it as a dependency, coupling server construction to the exploration layer's concrete class.

RelationUtils (api package) holds the canonical short-alias map for relation IDs — knowledge that logically belongs in config (where RelationConfig and RelationType live) but is placed in api to avoid upward coupling, creating a conceptual split in relation resolution logic.

QueryExecutor interface (query/QueryExecutor.java) has one concrete implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor) but is legitimately used for mock injection in SemanticFieldExplorer constructor and test harnesses — the interface earns its keep.
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens and CqlUtils.escapeForRegex are genuine shared primitives used by PatternSubstitution, RelationConfig, and GrammarConfigLoader, reducing real duplication across three classes.
HttpApiUtils enforces consistent policy (CORS headers, error JSON format, method validation) across all API handlers — not a pass-through, it provides uniform behavior.
RelationConfig contains a private static escapeForRegex(s) method at line 111-113 whose body is solely 'return CqlUtils.escapeForRegex(s)' — a pure pass-through wrapper with no added behavior.

PatternSubstitution.extractXposFromConstraint (lines 43-63) and RelationConfig.extractConstraintAttribute (lines 101-109) both parse CQL constraint strings to extract named attribute values (xpos/tag). PatternSubstitution uses indexOf string scanning; RelationConfig uses regex Pattern. These are parallel implementations of the same primitive that belong in CqlUtils.

SemanticFieldExplorer.getComparator() at line 289 exposes the internal CollocateProfileComparator collaborator directly; ExplorationHandlers reaches through it at line 177: 'semanticFieldExplorer.getComparator().compareCollocateProfiles(...)'. Callers bypass SemanticFieldExplorer's own interface to invoke the comparator.

ExplorationHandlers.buildExploreResponseBody and buildBaseExploreResponse are well-chosen private helpers that avoid response-building duplication across single-seed and multi-seed handlers — good consolidation.

Model classes (AdjectiveProfile, DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate records; ExplorationResult, ComparisonResult traditional classes) are appropriately scoped DTOs with no abstraction overhead. ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult include real derived-view methods (getFullyShared, getEdges, etc.) that justify their non-record form.
No delegation-heavy classes detected (mechanical scan confirms delegation_heavy_class_count = 0). No facade modules or typed-dict violations.
GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition deliberately extends CqlUtils bracket-walking to handle numbered label prefixes (1:[...] 2:[...]) — documented extension, not duplication.

BlackLabQueryExecutor has four public collocate-retrieval methods (findCollocations, executeBcqlQuery, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern), all following the same pipeline: build BCQL string → parse → get headword frequency → group hits → score with logDice → rank and return.

executeDependencyPattern (lines ~340-390) manually re-implements the parse/execute/group loop instead of reusing the private executeCollocateSearch helper that was extracted for findCollocations and executeSurfacePattern.

findCollocations mixes two distinct concerns: (1) constructing the BCQL query string from a CQL pattern (conditional %s-placeholder vs prefix-[ branch) and (2) executing the collocate search and scoring pipeline.

The three-phase pipeline in executeBcqlQuery (collectHits → scoreHits → sort) is cleanly separated with named private methods and record types (HitRecord, CollocateSearch), demonstrating that the design intent is good.

QueryExecutor interface Javadoc clearly distinguishes the four query methods, showing awareness of overlap; the design rationale is documented but the implementation does not fully enforce the separation.

BlackLabQueryExecutor.findCollocations (line ~132) and executeSurfacePattern (line ~437) each end with `catch (IOException e) { throw e; }` — a bare rethrow with no transformation, logging, or wrapping. These catch blocks are dead code: removing them leaves behaviour identical.

SemanticFieldExplorer.rankByLogDice (line ~240) does not rank — it filters by minShared, builds DiscoveredNoun objects, and returns an unsorted list. The caller immediately sorts the returned list by similarityScore. The method name implies an ordering contract the method does not fulfil.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed silently caps minShared: `int threshold = Math.min(minShared, seeds.size())`. ExplorationHandlers then echoes back the original uncapped minShared value in the API response (`buildBaseExploreResponse(..., minShared, ...)`). A caller passing min_shared=10 with 3 seeds gets threshold=3 applied but sees min_shared=10 in the response.
Guard-clause pattern used consistently throughout handlers: requireParam() → null check → return, numeric parse in try/catch → 400 → return. Control flow is clean and linear.

Multi-phase decomposition in BlackLabQueryExecutor (collectHits → scoreHits → sort/limit) and SemanticFieldExplorer (steps 1–4 with explicit logger.debug commentary) makes algorithmic intent traceable.
GrammarConfigLoader breaks parsing into granular private methods (parseRelations, parseRelation, validatePositions, deriveHeadPositionFromPattern, countPatternTokens) each with a single clear job.
handleFullSketchForType (surface branch) emits relation entries with keys {name, cql, collocate_pos_group, collocations} — no 'id', no 'total_matches'
handleRelationQueryForPattern (single surface relation) emits {id, name, collocations, total_matches} — no 'cql', no 'collocate_pos_group'
handleFullSketchForType (dep branch, via buildRelationResponse) emits {id, name, description, deprel, total_matches, collocations} — a third distinct shape

WordSketchApiServer constructor logs a warning when grammarConfig is null ('relation-based endpoints will return 500') yet immediately constructs ExplorationHandlers which throws IllegalArgumentException for null — the contract says degraded, the code crashes
/api/concordance/examples uses parameters word1/word2; /api/semantic-field/examples uses adjective/noun — both find example sentences for a word pair under different names
SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern has two public overloads: 3-arg (RelationConfig) and 5-arg (explicit strings) — both are public with no deprecation marker on the lower-level variant
QueryExecutor interface cleanly documents the distinction between findCollocations, executeQuery, executeBcqlQuery, and executeSurfacePattern with explicit @param contracts and cross-references
HttpApiUtils.requireParam follows a uniform null-return pattern and sendError/sendJsonResponse are used consistently throughout all handlers
buildBaseExploreResponse + buildExploreResponseBody are shared by both single-seed and multi-seed handlers, enforcing structural consistency for the exploration family

model package hosts standalone records/classes: AdjectiveProfile, DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, Edge, ExploreOptions, ExplorationResult, ComparisonResult — but WordSketchResult, CollocateResult, KwicResult, SnippetResult are nested inside QueryResults in the query package, splitting domain value objects across two homes with no documented rationale

ExploreParams (private record at line 382 of ExplorationHandlers.java) near-duplicates ExploreOptions (public record in model package): both bundle topCollocates, minShared, minLogDice, and a per-seed noun limit; they diverge only on one field (includeExamples vs nounsPerSeed)

API handler classes (ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, RelationUtils) are all package-private, while HttpApiUtils is public — a subtle access-modifier inconsistency among siblings in the same api package

ComparisonResult and ExplorationResult carry explicit Javadoc documenting the record-vs-class split as intentional; no equivalent rationale exists for why result types in QueryResults are nested rather than standalone

Class names are highly accurate throughout: BlackLabQueryExecutor, SemanticFieldExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator, GrammarConfigLoader, BlackLabSnippetParser all communicate their purpose precisely.
QueryExecutor interface precisely distinguishes findCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern, executeBcqlQuery — each name conveys the specific behavioural difference.
Record field names in model types are expressive: DiscoveredNoun.sharedCollocates(), CoreCollocate.sharedByCount(), AdjectiveProfile.commonalityScore(), AdjectiveProfile.distinctivenessScore().

CollocateProfileComparator exclusively handles adjectives: constant is ADJECTIVE_PATTERN, internal map is adjectiveProfiles, return type is AdjectiveProfile, but the class is named for 'collocate profiles' — name/behaviour mismatch at class level.

SemanticFieldExplorer.rankByLogDice does NOT rank: it scores candidate nouns (DiscoveredNoun) and filters by minShared, returning an unsorted list. The caller sorts. Name implies sorting as the primary action.

SemanticFieldExplorer uses abbreviated 'seedCollocScores' (Map<String, Double>) locally but the same data persists as ExplorationResult.seedCollocates — inconsistent colloc/collocate abbreviation across the same module.

ExploreOptions.nounsPerCollocate() and ExploreParams.nounsPerSeed() name the same tuning parameter differently; ExplorationHandlers assigns one to the other: int nounsPerCollocate = exploreParams.nounsPerSeed().

SemanticFieldExplorer.buildCollocateIndex builds Map<noun, Map<collocate, logDice>> — 'index' suggests a search structure; the result is a reverse-lookup profile map, making the name slightly misleading.
BlackLabQueryExecutor.getTotalFrequency uses tfl for TermFrequencyList — unexplained abbreviation inconsistent with the codebase's general preference for full variable names.
findCollocations uses posMap, executeDependencyPattern uses lemmaPosMap for the same concept (lemma → POS mapping) — parallel methods with divergent local variable vocabulary.

strengths: Strict top-down layer ordering with zero detected cycle pressure: utils → config/query → exploration → api → Main.
QueryExecutor interface properly abstracts the BlackLab backend; all callers depend on the interface, not the concrete class.
model package is a clean shared-DTO layer imported by both exploration and api without owning any logic.
indexer is fully decoupled from the rest of the runtime stack — no cross-wiring with query, api, or config at runtime.
CqlUtils in utils/ is already used as a shared primitive by both config and api, showing intent to keep low-level parsing unified.
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: Issues are localised duplication-of-boundary artefacts rather than structural violations: the layering is correct, but two places independently implement the same CQL-constraint merging logic because the natural home (utils/) was not extended when the second use-case emerged.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The codebase has a clear, consistently respected layered architecture with a proper interface boundary at the query layer and no import cycles — this places it well above the 60-point 'significant drag' anchor. Two issues pull the score below 80: (1) CQL constraint-building logic is duplicated across config.RelationConfig and api.PatternSubstitution, indicating a missing shared boundary that would require a small multi-file refactor to resolve; (2) WordSketchApiServer constructs SemanticFieldExplorer directly rather than accepting it by injection, hardwiring the composition seam. Together these represent bounded but real boundary blurring in an otherwise healthy architecture, landing at 72.

strengths: CqlUtils is a genuine shared primitive used by three distinct callers (PatternSubstitution, RelationConfig, GrammarConfigLoader), reducing real duplication at the exact right granularity.
HttpApiUtils enforces consistent policy (CORS, error serialization, method validation) rather than just delegating — every API endpoint benefits uniformly.
QueryExecutor interface, though single-impl, is genuinely used for mock injection in testing and is richly documented with distinct per-method responsibilities that map cleanly to real use-cases.
ExplorationHandlers.buildExploreResponseBody and buildBaseExploreResponse eliminate response-building duplication across single-seed and multi-seed flows without over-generalising.
Model records (AdjectiveProfile, DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate) are right-sized, with no unnecessary wrapping layers between domain logic and serialization.
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: Issues are localized to CQL string-manipulation helpers: one pass-through private wrapper and two parallel implementations of the same attribute-extraction primitive, plus one exposed-internal-collaborator pattern that lets callers bypass SemanticFieldExplorer's interface — none systemic.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The codebase's primary abstractions (QueryExecutor, GrammarConfigLoader, CqlUtils, HttpApiUtils, model layer) each deliver real leverage and pay for their indirection cost. The weaknesses are contained: RelationConfig.escapeForRegex is a no-value private wrapper, PatternSubstitution and RelationConfig independently implement CQL attribute parsing that belongs in CqlUtils, and SemanticFieldExplorer leaks its internal comparator. These reduce definition_directness locally but do not undermine the overall design. Against the global rubric (80 = solid but uneven), the score sits just below that threshold because the duplicated CQL attribute-extraction logic represents a missed abstraction opportunity in the one area (CQL string utilities) where shared infrastructure is actively being built.

strengths: Private helpers (executeCollocateSearch, buildAndRankCollocates, collectHits, scoreHits) correctly extract reusable pipeline stages, avoiding full duplication across at least two of the four public methods.
HitRecord and CollocateSearch records are purpose-built to carry only the data needed between phases, reducing parameter noise in the three-phase pipeline.
The QueryExecutor interface documents the conceptual distinctions between methods with precision, showing a deliberate design rather than accidental growth.
Single-responsibility is respected for smaller helpers: extractCollocateLemma, getTotalFrequency, close — each does exactly one thing.
design_coherence.issue_character: The issues are concentrated in one file and follow a single pattern: overlapping pipeline logic that was partially refactored but not completed, leaving executeDependencyPattern as a structural outlier that duplicates what the extracted helpers already do.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase sits solidly in the 65-75 range. The private helper extraction and record-based phase separation show genuine design discipline and keep most of the file coherent. However, the incomplete refactor — where executeDependencyPattern bypasses executeCollocateSearch and re-implements the same grouped-collocate pipeline — is a concrete design coherence defect, not just a style issue. Additionally, findCollocations mixes CQL string construction with execution, giving it two distinct responsibilities. These are real but localized issues; they do not indicate a systemic architectural problem across the module.

strengths: Guard-clause early-return pattern applied consistently across all HTTP handlers, keeping the happy path linear and branch-free.
Multi-phase algorithms are decomposed into named private methods with step comments, making the semantic-exploration algorithm easy to trace.
GrammarConfigLoader validation logic is split into small, single-purpose helpers with clear invariants (version check, legacy-key rejection, position validation).
Null/empty checks are precise and well-placed: empty-string guards at method entry, null-collocate paths handled explicitly rather than defaulting to 'unknown'.
The QueryExecutor interface Javadoc clearly distinguishes four overlapping methods (findCollocations, executeQuery, executeBcqlQuery, executeSurfacePattern) that would otherwise be easy to confuse.
logic_clarity.issue_character: Issues are isolated remnants and naming slip-ups rather than systemic design flaws: two dead catch-rethrow blocks left over from earlier refactoring, one misleadingly-named filter method, and one subtle parameter-reporting inconsistency in multi-seed mode.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase sits comfortably above 'significant drag' (60): the large majority of logic paths are clean, linear, and well-narrated, and there are no always-true/false conditions or identical branches. Two bare IOException rethrows in BlackLabQueryExecutor constitute dead code that adds reader noise; rankByLogDice's misnamed contract is a real confusion trap for anyone extending the exploration module; and the minShared cap/echo mismatch is a subtle correctness-adjacent clarity bug. These issues are genuine but localised, keeping the score below 'solid but uneven' (80).

strengths: QueryExecutor interface is thoroughly documented with explicit distinctions between the four query methods, cross-references between related methods, and precise @param contracts — a developer can understand which method to call and why
HttpApiUtils provides uniform sendJsonResponse/sendError/requireParam helpers used consistently across all handlers, eliminating ad-hoc per-endpoint HTTP boilerplate

Single-seed and multi-seed explore responses share buildBaseExploreResponse and buildExploreResponseBody, producing structurally compatible JSON shapes that a client can handle with the same parsing code
Handler classes (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers) have package-private visibility and clean delegation from WordSketchApiServer, keeping the public API boundary narrow
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: Issues are concentrated in the sketch-endpoint response shape family and one constructor null-contract mismatch — they are not randomly distributed but stem from incremental feature growth (dep added after surface, null-optional config never fully resolved) without normalising the shared response contract.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The exploration and utility layers are well-structured, but the HTTP API response shapes for the sketch family are visibly inconsistent: three distinct relation-entry schemas depending on which sketch variant you call, making client integration fragile. The constructor null contract for grammarConfig is actively misleading — it documents partial degradation but delivers an immediate crash. These are real developer-experience defects, not style nits. Against a global anchor of 80 for 'solid but uneven', the exploration cohesion and documented QueryExecutor pull upward while the multi-shape sketch responses and the false-safety constructor pull down to roughly 64.

strengths: Strict camelCase naming throughout all packages with zero snake_case drift — no naming convention collisions across the codebase
The intentional record-vs-class distinction in the model package is explicitly documented in ComparisonResult and ExplorationResult Javadoc, preventing false-positive confusion
All API handler siblings (ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, RelationUtils) follow the same structural pattern: package-private class, private final fields set via constructor, no public state

Private nested records for implementation details (HitRecord, CollocateSearch in BlackLabQueryExecutor; ExploreParams in ExplorationHandlers) are consistently used as local value bundles without leaking to the API surface
convention_outlier.issue_character: The convention issues are localized to two closely related phenomena: a split in where domain data types live (model package vs nested-in-QueryResults), and a partial duplication of an explore-parameters bundle record, suggesting the model package boundary was not revisited when QueryResults was introduced.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase is clean and consistent in naming, access modifiers, and structural patterns, placing it well above a 60 (significant drag) anchor. The main drag comes from a tangible split in where domain value objects live — result types in query.QueryResults vs the model package — which creates a navigation inconsistency developers will notice when adding new result types. The near-duplicate ExploreParams/ExploreOptions adds a second, smaller drift point. Together these pull the score below 80 (solid but uneven).

strengths: Class and interface names precisely reflect purpose: BlackLabQueryExecutor, SemanticFieldExplorer, GrammarConfigLoader, BlackLabSnippetParser are all self-documenting.

QueryExecutor interface method names clearly distinguish four distinct operations (findCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern, executeBcqlQuery), with Javadoc explaining each difference.
Model record fields (DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, AdjectiveProfile) use domain-accurate vocabulary: sharedByCount, commonalityScore, distinctivenessScore, seedLogDice.
The ComparisonResult Javadoc explicitly documents the get-prefix convention for non-record classes vs. record accessor convention, showing intentional naming consistency within types.

Phase helper methods in SemanticFieldExplorer (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateIndex, identifyCoreCollocates) follow a consistent verb-object naming scheme that makes the exploration pipeline easy to trace.
naming_quality.issue_character: Issues are concentrated in vocabulary divergence — the same concept receives different names across module boundaries (colloc vs collocate, nounsPerCollocate vs nounsPerSeed, collocate-profile vs adjective-profile) — rather than generic or meaningless names.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase clears the 80-point solid threshold at the class and interface level, where names are accurate and expressive. It is pulled below 80 by a consistent pattern of vocabulary drift across the exploration/comparison subsystem: CollocateProfileComparator is named for a broader abstraction than its adjective-only implementation, rankByLogDice misrepresents its primary behaviour (scoring and filtering, not sorting), and the colloc/collocate abbreviation splits the same concept across caller and callee in the same module. These issues are real friction for a developer navigating the exploration package but do not approach the pervasive problems that would justify a sub-70 score.

issues.summary: CQL constraint-merging logic is duplicated across config.RelationConfig and api.PatternSubstitution, revealing a missing shared boundary in utils/
issues.suggestion: Add a static CqlUtils.mergeLemmaIntoConstraint(String constraint, String lemma) method that extracts all attribute constraints (xpos, tag, deprel, etc.) and returns a reconstructed bracket with the lemma prepended. Replace both RelationConfig.mergeLemmaConstraint and PatternSubstitution's inline reconstruction with calls to this shared utility. This closes the boundary gap without any layer-direction change.
evidence: RelationConfig.mergeLemmaConstraint (line ~81) and RelationConfig.extractConstraintAttribute (line ~101) parse a CQL bracket, extract xpos/tag attributes, and rebuild a lemma-merged constraint.

PatternSubstitution.substituteCollocate (line ~16) and PatternSubstitution.extractXposFromConstraint (line ~43) do the same: parse a CQL bracket, extract xpos/tag, and rebuild a lemma-merged constraint.

Both implementations already share CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens as the token-splitting primitive, but the higher-level 'merge lemma into constraint' step was re-implemented rather than promoted to CqlUtils.

issues.summary: WordSketchApiServer instantiates SemanticFieldExplorer directly instead of accepting it via injection, coupling server construction to the exploration layer's concrete class
issues.suggestion: Add a SemanticFieldExplorer parameter to the WordSketchApiServer constructor and move the 'new SemanticFieldExplorer(executor)' call to Main.java. This shifts object composition to the wiring layer (Main) and lets the api package depend only on the already-injected instance, consistent with how SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers are already wired.
evidence: WordSketchApiServer constructor (line ~43): 'SemanticFieldExplorer semanticFieldExplorer = new SemanticFieldExplorer(executor);' — the concrete class is directly instantiated inside the api layer.
The instance is immediately passed to ExplorationHandlers, which already accepts it as a constructor parameter — so the injection pattern exists one level down but is bypassed at the top level.

Main.java constructs WordSketchApiServer with (executor, port, grammarConfig) — there is no opportunity to substitute a different SemanticFieldExplorer implementation without editing the server constructor.

issues.summary: Canonical relation-alias map lives in api.RelationUtils instead of config, splitting relation resolution knowledge across two packages
issues.suggestion: Move RELATION_ALIASES and resolveRelationAlias() into GrammarConfigLoader or a new config.RelationAliasRegistry class. api.RelationUtils can then either be removed or reduced to a thin delegation call. This keeps all relation-ID knowledge within the config boundary.
evidence: RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES maps short names ('adj_predicate', 'subject_of', etc.) to grammar-config IDs ('noun_adj_predicates', 'noun_verbs', etc.) — these IDs are defined by GrammarConfigLoader in the config package.
Any component outside the api layer (e.g., a CLI tool or a future gRPC layer) that needs alias resolution must either duplicate the map or take a dependency on the api package.
The placement is a deliberate workaround to avoid config importing api, but the correct fix is moving the map to config, not keeping it in the presentation layer.

issues.summary: CQL constraint attribute extraction is implemented twice with different strategies rather than once in CqlUtils
issues.suggestion: Add a public static String extractConstraintAttribute(String constraint, String attrName) method to CqlUtils that handles both xpos/tag and any attribute name using regex, then replace PatternSubstitution.extractXposFromConstraint and RelationConfig.extractConstraintAttribute with calls to it.
evidence: PatternSubstitution.extractXposFromConstraint (lines 43-63) uses indexOf string scanning to find 'xpos="...' or 'tag="...' and returns the raw attribute substring.
RelationConfig.extractConstraintAttribute (lines 101-109) uses java.util.regex.Pattern to match 'attrName="([^"]*)"' and returns 'attrName="value"'.
Both methods serve the same purpose (parse a CQL bracket expression and extract a named attribute value) and are called from analogous lemma-merging methods in their respective classes.
CqlUtils already houses the shared bracket-splitting and escape primitives that both files depend on — it is the natural home for a shared extractConstraintAttribute utility.

issues.summary: RelationConfig.escapeForRegex is a private pass-through wrapper with no added behavior over CqlUtils.escapeForRegex
issues.suggestion: Remove RelationConfig.escapeForRegex and replace the single call site at line 88 with a direct call to CqlUtils.escapeForRegex(headword).
evidence: RelationConfig lines 111-113: 'private static String escapeForRegex(String s) { return CqlUtils.escapeForRegex(s); }' — body is a single delegation, no added policy or translation.
The method is only called once internally (line 88: mergeLemmaConstraint). PatternSubstitution already calls CqlUtils.escapeForRegex directly without a wrapper (line 33).
The wrapper adds an indirection layer in the same package that already imports CqlUtils.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.getComparator() exposes internal collaborator, letting callers bypass the explorer's own interface
issues.suggestion: Add a compareCollocateProfiles delegation method on SemanticFieldExplorer that calls this.comparator.compareCollocateProfiles(...) and remove the getComparator() accessor. ExplorationHandlers would then call semanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles(...) without needing to know about CollocateProfileComparator.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer line 289: 'public CollocateProfileComparator getComparator() { return comparator; }' — raw accessor exposes the internal object.

ExplorationHandlers line 177: 'semanticFieldExplorer.getComparator().compareCollocateProfiles(nouns, minLogDice, maxPerNoun)' — callers chain through the accessor to invoke comparator behaviour directly, bypassing any centralised lifecycle or future policy SemanticFieldExplorer could apply.

The comparator was extracted from SemanticFieldExplorer to reduce class size (per JavaDoc), but the resulting design leaves ExplorationHandlers coupled to CollocateProfileComparator's interface unnecessarily.

issues.summary: executeDependencyPattern duplicates the grouped-collocate pipeline instead of reusing executeCollocateSearch
issues.suggestion: Refactor executeDependencyPattern to build its BCQL string, then delegate to executeCollocateSearch(bcql, lemma, false) + buildAndRankCollocates, eliminating the duplicated parse/execute/group block.
evidence: executeDependencyPattern manually calls CorpusQueryLanguageParser.parse, pattern.toQuery, blackLabIndex.search().find, searchHits.groupWithStoredHits — the same sequence that executeCollocateSearch already encapsulates.
executeCollocateSearch is used by findCollocations and executeSurfacePattern but not by executeDependencyPattern, so the BCQL-parse-execute-group flow exists in two places.

issues.summary: findCollocations mixes CQL-to-BCQL string construction with collocate search execution
issues.suggestion: Extract the CQL-to-BCQL string construction into a private static method (e.g., buildBcqlFromCqlPattern(lemma, cqlPattern)) so findCollocations only calls that helper and then delegates to executeCollocateSearch + buildAndRankCollocates.
evidence: The first ~20 lines of findCollocations inspect cqlPattern for %s or [ prefix and construct the bcql string via String.format — this is query-template logic.
The remainder of the method executes the search and returns ranked collocates — this is pipeline logic.
These two responsibilities share a method body with no extraction boundary between them.

issues.summary: Bare `catch (IOException e) { throw e; }` blocks in findCollocations and executeSurfacePattern are dead code
issues.suggestion: Remove both catch blocks entirely. If the intent was to distinguish IOException from other exceptions, document that in a comment; otherwise the bare rethrow only adds indirection noise.
evidence: findCollocations ends its try block with `} catch (IOException e) { throw e; }` — no logging, transformation, or wrapping; removing the catch leaves behaviour identical.
executeSurfacePattern contains the same pattern immediately before the CollocateSearch record declaration.

issues.summary: `rankByLogDice` in SemanticFieldExplorer neither ranks nor sorts — it filters and scores, leaving ranking to the caller
issues.suggestion: Rename to `filterCandidateNouns` or `buildCandidateNouns`, or move the sort inside the method and document that it returns a ranked list. Either way the name should match what the method actually does.
evidence: rankByLogDice (line ~240) iterates nounProfiles, filters by minShared, and returns an unsorted ArrayList of DiscoveredNoun objects.
The caller immediately calls `discoveredNouns.sort((a, b) -> Double.compare(b.similarityScore(), a.similarityScore()))` after the return — the ranking happens outside the method.
The method name implies an ordering contract (`rank`) that the method does not fulfil; a reader would expect the returned list to be sorted.

issues.summary: exploreMultiSeed silently caps minShared to seeds.size() but the API response echoes back the original uncapped value
issues.suggestion: Either log a warning when capping occurs (`if (minShared > seeds.size()) logger.warn(...)`) and echo the effective threshold in the response, or enforce the cap in the handler before calling exploreMultiSeed so both the logic and the response use the same value.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed: `int threshold = Math.min(minShared, seeds.size())` caps the effective threshold without logging the cap.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti calls `buildBaseExploreResponse(relationType, topCollocates, minShared, ...)` which puts the original uncapped minShared into the `parameters.min_shared` response field.
A caller with 3 seeds passing min_shared=10 sees `"min_shared": 10` in the response but threshold=3 was actually applied.

issues.summary: Three incompatible relation-entry shapes across the sketch endpoint family
issues.suggestion: Define a canonical relation-entry shape (id, name, collocations, total_matches, and optionally cql/deprel) and use it in all three paths. Extract a static buildRelationEntry(RelationConfig, List<WordSketchResult>, List<Map<String,Object>>) helper in SketchHandlers that both handleFullSketchForType and handleRelationQueryForPattern call, so the JSON structure is guaranteed identical regardless of how the endpoint is invoked.
evidence: Full surface sketch (handleFullSketchForType, surface branch): relation entry has {name, cql, collocate_pos_group, collocations} — no 'id', no 'total_matches'
Single surface relation (handleRelationQueryForPattern, SURFACE): relation entry has {id, name, collocations, total_matches} — no 'cql', no 'collocate_pos_group'
Full dep sketch (buildRelationResponse): entry has {id, name, description, deprel, total_matches, collocations} — a third distinct shape

issues.summary: WordSketchApiServer constructor misleadingly warns about null grammarConfig but immediately crashes
issues.suggestion: Resolve the contract in one of two ways: (a) make grammarConfig a required parameter — replace the warn with Objects.requireNonNull(grammarConfig, "grammarConfig is required") and remove the null branch entirely; or (b) if null is genuinely intended to mean 'no grammar available', change ExplorationHandlers to also tolerate null by returning 503/501 from its handlers rather than throwing at construction time. Option (a) is simpler and matches how ExplorationHandlers already behaves.
evidence: WordSketchApiServer constructor: if (grammarConfig == null) { logger.warn("...relation-based sketch/relations endpoints will return 500") } — implies server starts in degraded mode
Same constructor, next line: this.explorationHandlers = new ExplorationHandlers(grammarConfig, semanticFieldExplorer) — ExplorationHandlers throws IllegalArgumentException for null grammarConfig
The warn message and the crash are mutually exclusive outcomes; the constructor cannot deliver on its stated contract

issues.summary: Two similar 'find examples for a word pair' endpoints use inconsistent parameter names
issues.suggestion: Standardise on a shared naming convention for the two-token input. The simplest option is to keep the domain-specific names (adjective/noun where the endpoint is explicitly about adjective-noun pairs, word1/word2 for the generic concordance) but document the asymmetry clearly in the endpoint comment. Alternatively, accept both word1/word2 and adjective/noun as aliases in the semantic-field/examples handler so callers can use the same parameterisation across endpoints.
evidence: /api/concordance/examples requires word1 and word2 query parameters
/api/semantic-field/examples requires adjective and noun query parameters
Both endpoints retrieve corpus sentences illustrating a word-pair collocation; a client calling both must know a different parameter name for the same concept

issues.summary: Domain result records live in query.QueryResults instead of the model package, splitting data-type ownership across two homes
issues.suggestion: Move WordSketchResult, CollocateResult, KwicResult, and SnippetResult (and the ConcordanceResult sealed interface) to the model package as standalone top-level types, matching the convention established by AdjectiveProfile and DiscoveredNoun. Retain QueryResults only as a thin utility class or delete it.
evidence: WordSketchResult, CollocateResult, KwicResult, SnippetResult are nested records inside QueryResults in the query package
AdjectiveProfile, DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, Edge, ExploreOptions are standalone public records in the model package
No Javadoc or comment explains why query-layer results are co-located in QueryResults rather than placed in model like their siblings

issues.summary: ExploreParams (private record in ExplorationHandlers) near-duplicates ExploreOptions (public record in model), both bundling the same exploration tuning parameters
issues.suggestion: Eliminate ExploreParams by having parseExploreParams return an ExploreOptions directly. Reconcile the one divergent field (nounsPerSeed vs nounsPerCollocate) in ExploreOptions and add the includeExamples default inline at the call site.
evidence: ExploreParams at line 382 of ExplorationHandlers: record ExploreParams(int topCollocates, int minShared, double minLogDice, int nounsPerSeed)
ExploreOptions in model package: record ExploreOptions(int topCollocates, int nounsPerCollocate, double minLogDice, int minShared, boolean includeExamples)
Four of the five fields overlap; the handler converts ExploreParams into ExploreOptions before calling SemanticFieldExplorer

issues.summary: CollocateProfileComparator exclusively handles adjectives but its name implies generic collocate profiles
issues.suggestion: Rename the class to AdjectiveProfileComparator and its method to compareAdjectiveProfiles to match implementation vocabulary; or, if generic collocate comparison is planned, parameterise the POS pattern and update field names accordingly.
evidence: ADJECTIVE_PATTERN constant '[xpos="JJ.*"]' hard-codes adjective-only lookup
buildRawAdjectiveProfiles returns Map<adjective, Map<noun, logDice>> — no other POS is handled
compareCollocateProfiles returns ComparisonResult containing List<AdjectiveProfile>
Class name says 'Collocate' but implementation vocabulary is entirely 'Adjective'

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.rankByLogDice scores and filters candidates but does not rank them
issues.suggestion: Rename to scoreAndFilterCandidates or buildScoredNounCandidates to accurately describe scoring + filtering behaviour.
evidence: Method body creates DiscoveredNoun objects with a similarityScore and filters by minShared threshold
Returns an unsorted List<DiscoveredNoun>; caller (exploreByPattern) performs sort after
Method name 'rankByLogDice' implies sorting as the primary action, but sort is delegated to caller

issues.summary: Same parameter named nounsPerCollocate in ExploreOptions but nounsPerSeed in ExploreParams, causing a cross-naming assignment
issues.suggestion: Align both records on one name. 'nounsPerCollocate' better describes the algorithmic meaning (nouns fetched per collocate in reverse-lookup phase); rename ExploreParams.nounsPerSeed to nounsPerCollocate.
evidence: ExploreOptions record field: int nounsPerCollocate()
ExploreParams private record field: int nounsPerSeed()
ExplorationHandlers line: int nounsPerCollocate = exploreParams.nounsPerSeed() — explicit rename at boundary

issues.summary: Abbreviation 'colloc' used for local variables in SemanticFieldExplorer but 'collocate' used in persisted ExplorationResult fields for the same data
issues.suggestion: Rename local variables seedCollocScores → collocateScores (or seedCollocateScores) and seedCollocFrequencies → collocateFrequencies to match ExplorationResult field vocabulary.
evidence: Local variables: seedCollocScores (Map<String,Double>), seedCollocFrequencies (Map<String,Long>)
ExplorationResult fields: seedCollocates, seedCollocateFrequencies — full word used
SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern: Map<String, Double> seedCollocScores; Map<String, Long> seedCollocFrequencies — then passed to ExplorationResult constructor which names them differently

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.buildCollocateIndex returns a Map<noun, Map<collocate, logDice>> but 'index' implies a search structure
issues.suggestion: Rename to buildNounCollocateProfiles or buildReverseCollocateMap to describe the data structure accurately.
evidence: Method signature: private Map<String, Map<String, Double>> buildCollocateIndex(...)
Method body builds a reverse-lookup map from noun to its collocate scores, not a search index
'Index' has an established meaning in this codebase (Lucene/BlackLab index); using it for a plain nested Map creates vocabulary collision
